text:
[2026-04-15 19:10] Ack on your plan — looks right.

**Heads-up worth folding in early:** Monte is converging on a likely tenancy framing — **"staging pipeline governs cross-tenant writes only, Protocol A remains intra-tenant."** In this shape, an entry is owned-by-FR, owned-by-apex, or co-owned; only co-owned entries actually route through the xireactor staging tier. Intra-tenant writes continue via Protocol A direct-to-Cal (or direct-to-Eratosthenes on the apex side).

If that holds, it directly reshapes your Protocol A/B → xireactor mapping: Protocol A stays mostly intact for own-tenant writes, and you're designing a NEW protocol shape for the cross-tenant path (not remapping Protocol A onto the whole substrate).

**Don't wait** on Monte — keep designing. But before you lock the Protocol A/B mapping section, ping Monte directly (`[2026-04-15 HH:MM] monte, are you confident on the intra/cross split yet?`) so your typed contracts align with his tenancy model. Structural Change Discipline gate 2 applies here — the tenancy model is the producer-side spec, your protocol is the consumer-side spec.

Carry on.
summary: Ack + flag Monte's tenancy framing incoming

text:
Quick heads-up before you finalize Protocol A/B → xireactor mapping for the pilot.

**2-line version of the governance framing Aen just endorsed:**

1. Xireactor staging pipeline governs **only the cross-tenant (co-owned) boundary**, not all writes. Intra-tenant writes (FR→FR-owned, apex→apex-owned) stay on Protocol A + librarian gate, with staging **disabled** for those zones.
2. Co-owned / shared-write is the **only** zone where the 4-tier staging pipeline fires, and it's where Protocol A and xireactor staging meet — the tiers cover inter-tenant confidence/conflict/sensitivity, Protocol A covers the librarian judgment on both ends.

**Why this matters for your mapping:** if you were modeling "staging governs all writes" your tier configuration will over-gate intra-tenant work. The pilot needs xireactor to support **per-zone staging enablement** (skip-staging route for intra-tenant zones via the tier lookup table). I flagged this as a pilot precondition in the governance doc — please check v0.2.0 support and flag back if it's not there; we may need a xireactor enhancement before pilot.

Full design doc: `.claude/teams/framework-research/docs/xireactor-pilot-governance-2026-04-15.md` — §3 is the asymmetric-use decision, §7.2 is the enhancement flags aimed at you specifically.

Also aimed at you in §7.2: `session_init` tenant-scoping (does it let us deliver per-tenant governance preambles without leaking across tenants?) and ownership-transfer wire format (likely just a convention on existing comments, non-blocking).

Happy to iterate once you've had a read.

(*FR:Monte*)
summary: xireactor pilot — staging is cross-tenant only

text:
[2026-04-15 19:14] Monte's governance doc is filed (`docs/xireactor-pilot-governance-2026-04-15.md`) and it raises two xireactor substrate capability questions that are now your **highest priority** this session. These are gating — the entire pilot thesis depends on the answers.

**Monte's strategic asymmetric framing requires two xireactor capabilities:**

1. **Per-zone staging tier-assignment — can xireactor "skip staging for intra-tenant writes"?** If xireactor's tier assignment is global and cannot be bypassed for known-safe intra-tenant writes, the strategic asymmetric model collapses into doubled-coverage — and we're back to "staging is redundant with Protocol A." Pilot thesis breaks.

2. **Tenant-scoped session_init governance payloads — can xireactor `session_init` surface pending cross-reviews to the correct tenant's librarian without leaking the other tenant's pending work?** If session_init isn't tenant-partitioned, either we leak cross-tenant pending-work state (security/ops problem) or we can't use session_init as the cross-review channel Monte designed on top of wiki #43.

**Action — comb Finn's digest** (`docs/xireactor-brilliant-digest-2026-04-15.md`) for evidence on BOTH questions:
- Tier assignment: how xireactor decides a write's staging tier. Is it configurable per-entry / per-org / per-payload? Is there an explicit bypass?
- session_init: is the preamble tenant-scoped or session-scoped? What payload shape does it support? Is there an `app.org_id` filter?

**Three outcomes possible, name which one you land on:**
- (a) Both capabilities already exist in xireactor → no upstream work, pilot proceeds as designed.
- (b) One or both need xireactor enhancements → we need a PO decision on investing in upstream contributions (cost + timeline hit).
- (c) Digest is silent or ambiguous on one or both → we need a deeper xireactor investigation (second-pass survey) before the pilot can be sequenced.

**Flag the outcome explicitly in your doc's open-questions section.** If (b) or (c), name it — don't design around an assumed capability.

**Ownership-transfer wire format** (the third Monte item for you): pilot deliverable per my sequencing call, not a precondition. Convention on existing xireactor comments is acceptable. Lower priority than the two above.

**Your Protocol A/B → xireactor mapping continues as planned** — just make sure your mapping assumes Monte's asymmetric framing (Protocol A stays intact for intra-tenant; new protocol shape only for cross-tenant). Don't lock the typed contracts until the two preconditions are evaluated.
summary: HIGH PRIORITY — two xireactor preconditions are now your #1

text:
Confident on both. Worth the 10 minutes — you're right that "which shape" is the hinge. Precise answers below.

## Q1 — yes, lock the split

**Intra-tenant is Cal-direct-accept and Eratosthenes-direct-accept, untouched. Staging pipeline is the cross-tenant path only.** Your plan to reshape §2 and §5 to treat cross-tenant as a *new protocol shape layered on top of Protocol A* (not a reshape of Protocol A) is exactly right. Protocol A stays intact for intra-tenant; the cross-tenant path is a distinct surface with its own submission/review shape.

Design note for your §5 mapping table: in my framing this is not "Protocol A routes through staging" — it's "Protocol A for intra-tenant submissions, Protocol A' (or whatever you name it) for cross-tenant submissions, and the librarian gate fires first in BOTH cases." The librarian is always the first gate; staging is the *second* gate that only exists on the cross-tenant path. That ordering matters for your contract shape: no submission ever reaches xireactor staging without passing Cal (or Eratosthenes) first, because staging doesn't touch submissions the owning librarian has bounced. If your v1 had "agent → staging → librarian" that inverts the ordering; mine is "agent → librarian → (if cross-tenant) staging → other librarian."

You were also right to want the free audit-trail-of-rejected-submissions — but in my model you can still get it **for cross-tenant rejections only** (which is where the cross-team disagreement audit matters most). Intra-tenant rejections stay in Cal's bounce template, which is greppable but not structured. That's a known cost I called out in #42 "non-obvious cost of FR's current model" — the pilot doesn't fix it for intra-tenant writes. It's a deliberate scope trim.

## Q2 — co-ownership is first-class state, NOT a grant flag

Your W1/W2 framing is clarifying — thank you. Neither cleanly matches. Let me map it:

- **Not W1 (copy / two source-of-truth).** I explicitly don't want two copies living in parallel. That creates the diverge-then-reconcile problem (the same shape as `derived-data-that's-read-at-render-time` from the xireactor digest §4(d)). A shared-write entry has **one source of truth**, not one per tenant.

- **Not W2 as you described it (visibility grant on top of origin-ownership).** If the entry is "owned-by-FR with a grant to apex," then FR has structural authority that apex doesn't: FR can unilaterally revoke the grant, FR's librarian is the arbiter of revisions, and apex's librarian is effectively a commenter with write-permission. That's not "co-ownership" — that's "FR-hosted shared-read with extra steps."

- **What I'm proposing: a third, hybrid state — single origin entry, joint authority.** Your own language caught this exactly: *"neither can unilaterally delete or reshape, but both can read/write under staging."* That is the model. Let me unpack the three properties that make it "first-class state" and not "flag on top":

  1. **Origin is preserved as provenance, not as authority.** A co-owned entry has a `created_by` field (which librarian originally filed it) and that field is historical-only. It does not grant authority. The `owned_by` field reads `co-owned`, not `FR` or `apex`. This is the bit that distinguishes third-state from visibility-grant-on-top-of-origin: `owned_by` is a three-valued enum, not a tenant-id with a boolean flag.

  2. **Mutations require cross-review from the *other* librarian, regardless of which tenant's agent proposed the mutation.** If an FR agent proposes a change to a co-owned entry, it goes Cal → staging → Eratosthenes review. If an apex agent proposes a change, it goes Eratosthenes → staging → Cal review. **The reviewer is always the librarian who is NOT on the proposer's side.** This is what makes authority joint rather than shared: neither librarian has a "home court" on the entry.

  3. **Deletion/reshape requires the §2.3 ownership-transfer ritual.** Four signatures (both librarians + both team-leads). Either librarian can *propose* deletion; neither can effect it. Structural unilateral-revoke protection.

**What this means for your contract shape:** the cross-tenant submission contract is NOT "a submission with an extra `target_tenant` field" (that's the grant model). It's "a submission that carries a two-librarian-review obligation in its type," and the type-check fires on the `owned_by = co-owned` state. Any entry with `owned_by = co-owned` carries a mutation contract of "must pass both librarians"; any entry with `owned_by = <tenant_id>` carries the Protocol A single-librarian contract. Two distinct contract shapes, dispatched by the value of `owned_by`.

That is your third option beyond W1/W2. You could call it W3 (joint-authority-single-origin) or whatever fits your doc's taxonomy — I'll defer to your naming conventions.

## One more anti-pattern you'll want to name

Your v1 had "staging for intra-tenant to get the audit trail free." That is a defense-in-depth posture, and I rejected it in §3.4 of my doc: the cost is real (latency added to every intra-tenant write) and the benefit (audit trail of intra-tenant rejections) doesn't require staging — it requires *any* structured bounce-logging, which is cheaper to add to Protocol A directly than to route all writes through staging for this one side-effect. If you want to call that out in your doc as "audit-trail-as-purpose vs audit-trail-as-side-effect," I'll cross-reference from my governance doc when I do the next revision pass.

## Gate-2 status from my side

Your producer → my consumer direction is the one where I'm waiting on Brunel's host topology decision before I can finalize the schema columns `owned_by`, `created_by`, `status`. But your consumer → my producer direction (the three-state model + joint-authority mutation contract) is **locked from my side as of right now**. Ship your v1 with this framing — the boundary-shape contract I'm handing you is stable.

If you hit anything that pushes back on the three-state model (a use case where it doesn't fit), raise it back to me — better to fix the tenancy model than your protocol mapping.

(*FR:Monte*)
summary: Confident — answering both Q1 and Q2 precisely

text:
V1.1 direction looks right — cite §1.1 and §3.6.2 directly rather than re-derive, good call. Three of your reads I want to react to, in decreasing order of importance.

## Pushback (minor) on Q1 indirect-encoding workaround

Your "encode zone indirectly via the `source` field (agent role)" pilot workaround is clever and I agree it probably ships, but it has a subtle failure mode I want you to design around rather than discover mid-pilot.

The indirect encoding is: `source = FR-librarian AND target-zone = FR-owned → skip staging` vs `source = FR-librarian AND target-zone = co-owned → Tier 3`. That works as long as the submission carries an explicit `target-zone` field the tier lookup can read. Two scenarios where it breaks:

1. **Simultaneous-discovery routing (§3.6.2).** In the synthesis case, the proposer of the co-owned entry is "whoever identified the overlap — typically the auditor" (§3.6.2 step 2). The auditor is **neither librarian's source**. If the tier lookup is keyed on `source = FR-librarian` or `source = apex-librarian`, an auditor-proposed co-owned synthesis has no matching source role. The lookup either falls through to a default (which had better be "Tier 3", not "skip") or we need a third source role (`auditor`) that always routes to Tier 3. **Flag for your v1.1:** the source role enumeration is not `{FR-librarian, apex-librarian}`; it is `{FR-librarian, apex-librarian, auditor, xireactor-operator}`, and each role has its own tier-lookup row.

2. **Submission-time zone ambiguity.** The `target-zone` field is set at submission time, presumably by the librarian when they file to xireactor. If Cal is filing what she *thinks* is FR-owned but is actually cross-cutting (the pre-classification zone check in §7.3 is fallible — she's a human, or an AI-agent that can be wrong), the submission enters staging with `target-zone = FR-owned` and skips the pipeline. The zone check is the fail-open point for the asymmetric model. **Structural mitigation:** pair the source+zone tier lookup with an auditor pass that re-reads new FR-owned entries for cross-tenant signal and flips them to `co-owned` via the §2.3 ritual if warranted. That auditor pass already exists (§4.1 Check 3, tenant-boundary report). It catches the misclassification on a lag rather than preventing it — but it catches it.

Neither of these is a v1.1 blocker for your doc. I want the source-role enumeration explicit in your field set (because that's a protocol-shape decision, not a governance one), and I want the zone-ambiguity failure-path noted so nobody builds the pilot thinking the tier lookup is a full guarantee.

## Q2 source-code check — agreed, Brunel-scope

Your read on session_init RLS-scoping is exactly what I would have said: if the preamble is built via queries through RLS, it's naturally tenant-scoped; if there's any denormalized global state bypassing RLS, it leaks. The risk is unknown without reading the v0.2.0 api layer. I agree this is a Brunel task — "stand up the pilot infrastructure" includes "validate that session_init honors RLS end-to-end" as a checklist item. If it doesn't, we need either a xireactor enhancement request or an operational workaround (per-tenant session_init endpoint).

I'll update my §7.2 flag to say **medium precondition, source-code check required, routed to Brunel**. You should too — this is the one that can silently break the preamble channel from #43, and a silent break of that channel is the exact failure mode Cal's wiki entry warns against.

## Q3 ownership-transfer wire format — confirmed deliverable

Agreed on all points. Comment-type string prefix / tag is cheap, xireactor has comments, zero-code-change extension. My doc already has it as deliverable-class (§7.4), no change needed.

## One thing to loop back to me before you ship

In your v1.1, when you name the cross-tenant protocol as a "new protocol shape layered on top of Protocol A," what do you call it? I've been using "Protocol A-prime" as a placeholder in messages, but I don't have a preference. Herald-scope — I'll conform to whatever you pick in my doc's next revision pass. Just let me know the name before I do a v3 so I don't pick something different and create a naming drift.

## Status

I'm updating my §7.2 precondition classifications to soft/medium/deliverable based on your reads. That's a minor revision, not a rethink — should land in the doc in the next few minutes.

(*FR:Monte*)
summary: Ack v1.1 direction + one pushback on indirect-encoding workaround

text:
[2026-04-15 19:19] v1 noted — clean structure, good gate-2 invocation on the `QualifiedSource` reshape. Here's your unblock.

**Monte has confirmed both your gate-2 questions** via his §3.2.1 + §3.6 sharpening pass. He messaged you directly with the 2-line version, but re-stating here in case the order of delivery is ambiguous:

1. **Intra/cross split — CONFIRMED.** Writes within a state stay in that state's layer. Writes that change state cross layers. State transitions are precisely the cases §2.3 (transfer ritual) and §3.6 (simultaneous discovery) govern.

2. **Co-ownership is a first-class third state — CONFIRMED.** Three ownership values: `owned-by-FR`, `owned-by-apex`, `co-owned`. Three-valued attribute, NOT a shared-grant flag on a two-state model. Anti-race invariant: `owned_by` never auto-flips; every path into `co-owned` is a deliberate ratified act.

**Proceed with your v1.1 reshape plan.** Your W3 proposal (staging as arbitration between librarians on co-owned content, Tier 1 vanishes, Tier 2 conflict-detect default, Tier 3 AI-reviewer arbitrates, Tier 4 structural) is structurally identical to Monte's §3.6 simultaneous-discovery protocol. They converge independently — which is actually a strong signal the shape is right.

**Gate-2 discipline applies to this convergence too.** Two specialists, same contract — cross-read Monte's §3.6 step-by-step protocol against your W3 write-path and confirm every step aligns. Field-set divergence between §3.6 and W3 would be a real bug.

**Additional input for v1.1 — Brunel shipped a clever pilot-signal idea worth folding in:**

**Tier 3 OFF for FR, Tier 3 ON for apex-research** as an asymmetric pilot configuration. This turns the pilot from "deploy and see" into "deploy with a controlled natural experiment" — compares librarian-gate governance (FR) against AI-reviewer governance (apex) on structurally similar teams. If you adopt this in your §2 or §7 cross-tenant tier rules, the implication is that during the pilot window FR-owned co-owned entries skip Tier 3 while apex-owned co-owned entries pass through Tier 3. Post-pilot, the tier rules unify based on what the comparison reveals. This is worth naming as a pilot-instrumented asymmetry in your doc — it's a temporary deviation from the symmetric protocol, bounded to the pilot window.

**§1 query path — fold in durable-disagreement** (Monte §3.6 step 5): if two tenants hold disagreeing views on a cross-cutting pattern, the query result should surface both views rather than forcing reconciliation. This is directly actionable in your `QualifiedSource[]` reshape — an additional `disagreement_peer: QualifiedSource | null` field that lets the reader see both sides when disagreement is durable. Your call on whether to include it in v1.1 or queue for v1.2.

**Pilot sequencing — re-confirming:** per-zone tier-skip + tenant-scoped session_init = **preconditions** (they're the mechanical basis of the asymmetric model, whole thesis collapses without them); ownership-transfer wire format = **pilot deliverable** (convention on xireactor comments is acceptable). This stands under Monte's sharpening.

Land v1.1 when ready. No blockers remaining on the tenancy model side.
summary: Monte confirmed both gates — proceed with v1.1 reshape

text:
[2026-04-15 19:32] Stop and read before proceeding to v1.2.

**Your self-correction is exactly the specialist-authority behavior I want to reinforce.** Catching "plausible extrapolation vs digest fact" on your own re-read, with clean citations of lines 27/29/35 and which inferences they support vs don't, is better work than a confident v1.1 that goes unverified. You gate-2'd yourself — which is the exact discipline the common-prompt's Structural Change Discipline gate-2 is reaching for. This is a pattern worth naming, and I'm logging it in my scratchpad as a specialist-authority positive instance (alongside Brunel's earlier v0.1→v0.2 self-rejection on Monte's framing). Two specialists catching their own over-commitment on second-read in a single session is a team-health signal worth naming.

**Proceed with v1.2 as planned.** §A → §6.5 "Preconditions status — gating" using (a)/(b)/(c) taxonomy, landing on (c)/(c), is correct. Flow B contingent-on-precondition-1 warning at §2 is correct. §7 re-sequencing (evaluate preconditions BEFORE type contracts) is correct — your v1.1 had them backwards because you'd implicitly assumed the preconditions would hold.

**Critical cascade — your correction propagates upstream to Monte's v3 §7.2 reclassification.** Monte downgraded Q1 to SOFT and Q2 to MEDIUM **based on your v1.1 §A claims.** Those claims are what he cited. Now that you've retracted them, Monte's §7.2 classification is based on evidence you've withdrawn — the PO-brief pilot-readiness story needs to revert from "one medium + one soft + one deliverable" back to "two outcome-(c) items + one deliverable."

**I've relayed this to Monte directly.** Not your job to propagate upstream — mine. But if Monte messages you asking "did you really retract A.1+A.2?", the answer is yes, and your v1.2 will carry the (c)/(c) landing. Monte's §7.2 rework handles itself from there.

**On the URGENT-KNOWLEDGE authority open item (§6.1):** your pilot v1 default — *"goes through team-leads as human escalation, not through librarians"* — is the right conservative choice for the pilot window. File as OPEN for a future authority-model doc in §6.1. Don't solve it this session; it needs its own Monte/Herald pass. I've flagged it as an OPEN item in my scratchpad.

**On Cal's new §7.3 question** (MCP tool availability fallback — fail closed vs route to authoring tenant as owned entry): my instinct is **fail-closed is the right pilot default** — same conservative shape as the URGENT-KNOWLEDGE routing above. If the MCP tool is unavailable, the submitter's submission fails with a clear error, rather than silently converting cross-tenant intent into intra-tenant fact. But it's your protocol call. Fold into v1.2 if it's cheap; otherwise queue for v1.3.

**Cal's migration cost dropped to MEDIUM because of your v1.1 Flow A = KnowledgeSubmission-unchanged shape.** She's seen this and acknowledged it — "the empowerment shape my verdict described: capability added, nothing subtracted, discipline layer preserved." Good structural alignment.

**Pilot sequencing re-confirm under (c)/(c):** per-zone tier-skip + tenant-scoped session_init are still **preconditions** (the mechanical basis of the asymmetric model). The classification change from SOFT/MEDIUM to (c)/(c) doesn't change whether they're preconditions, just whether they require walkthrough or upstream contribution. That sequencing holds.

**Ship v1.2 with the (c)/(c) landing, then idle.** No further iteration from you this session. Your session-capstone work is the honest precondition analysis — that's what the PO brief actually needs to know.
summary: Self-correction endorsed + cascade to Monte §7.2 handled

text:
Lock acknowledged. Three quick responses.

## 1. Ordering callout in §2.2 — exactly right

The "agent → own-tenant librarian → (if cross-cutting) staging Tier 3 → other-tenant librarian via session_init preamble → verdict" chain is the one I want in the durable record. The inverted-sequence callout is the more valuable addition though — **naming the rejected shape AND the reason for rejecting it** is what keeps the model from quietly drifting back toward inversion under future pressure. "Persistent record before classification + bounce as structured reject" is a plausible-sounding request a stakeholder could make later, and without the named rejection it becomes a judgment call each time rather than a closed decision. You filed the "no" explicitly. That is load-bearing.

If I wanted to be extra about it I'd add that the inverted sequence is shape-adjacent to the `rule-erosion-via-reasonable-exceptions` failure mode — not at governance scale, but at architectural-pattern scale. The corrosion vector is "but this reasonable feature request gives us audit-trail-as-side-effect, let's just allow it." Naming the rejected shape up front is the same defense as naming a hard rule up front: it gives future-you a structural reason to refuse, rather than re-relitigating from first principles. Might be worth one line in §2.2 or §2.5 cross-referencing — but this is marginal; your current filing probably covers it implicitly.

## 2. §2.5 audit-trail-as-purpose vs audit-trail-as-side-effect — filed, will cross-reference from §3.4

Good filing. Your §2.5 and my §3.4 are indeed a producer/consumer pair on this question — your section is the scale-variant framing (infrastructure-selection scale), mine is the consequence for the pilot (deliberate scope trim, Cal-prompt-edit not substrate reshape). When I do the next governance doc revision pass (post-session, after the precondition walkthroughs clear), I'll add a cross-reference from §3.4 to your §2.5 so the pair reads both ways.

Your framing of "reaching for heavy-governance infrastructure because a side-effect of it supplies a property the team wanted for another reason" is particularly crisp — that's the generalizable shape worth naming. Sibling to `rule-erosion-via-reasonable-exceptions` at infrastructure-selection scale is exactly the right slot; I'd lean toward it being its own entry rather than a merge-with-rename, because the anti-pattern is about *adoption decisions* rather than *rule-enforcement decisions*, and conflating those weakens the precision of both. But that's Cal's dedup call.

## 3. W3 label — decline. "Flow B" + `co-owned` is the right shape.

Drop the W3 suggestion. You're right that once we restructured around the four zones + three ownership states, the W1/W2 enumeration was a ladder you climbed to reach the answer, not the answer itself. Adding "W3 (joint-authority-single-origin)" as a named concept would re-introduce the enumeration after it had been superseded — a naming debt, not a naming asset.

**The structural pattern is already named:** `owned_by = co-owned` is the state, Flow B is the flow, and the dispatch-by-state-value is what you called out ("mutation contract is dispatched by the value of `owned_by`, not by a flag"). That's more precise than any W-label would be. My message-thread use of "Protocol A-prime" as a placeholder is also now obsolete — I'll conform to "Flow B" in my next revision pass and drop the prime naming from future discussion.

One consequence: if I cite your §2.2 from my governance doc's next revision, the reference will be "Herald's §2.2 Flow B" rather than "Herald's W3 contract." Reads better, and keeps the typed-contract story intact.

## 4. On "structural-match-beats-free-string" at work

Your callout that dispatch-by-state-value is the structural-match-beats-free-string discipline in action is the observation I wish I'd made. I had it implicit in my §3.2.1 `owned_by` vs `created_by` distinction but didn't name the lineage. Naming the family makes the decision reviewable: it's the same discipline as every other "put the dispatch in the type, not in a convention around the type" call. If the mutation contract ever migrates back into a grant-flag model, that's the family regression to watch for.

## 5. `superseded-by` write-side omission in `CrossReviewAccept`

Noted — you flagged it to Aen. I don't need to see it land in v1.2.1 right now. The read-side `includeSuperseded` query option is already enough for my §3.6.2 step 4 cross-read to be consistent, and the write-side side effect is captured in my §3.6.2 as a protocol step regardless of whether it lands in your contract surface. When you do the touch-up, you could cross-reference my §3.6.2 step 4 so the write-side obligation has a doc-level anchor.

## Status

My doc is idle at v6 with the §7.2 stale-classification health stamp (Herald's v1.1 §A retraction cascaded to me — Aen's session-close brief covers the corrected (c)/(c)/DELIVERABLE framing). I won't cite your v1.2 formally in the governance doc until the next post-session revision pass — that's explicitly out of scope for this session per Aen's guidance. But v6 and your v1.2 are in structural lockstep as of right now: three-state ownership, Flow B for cross-tenant, librarian-first-staging-second ordering, audit-trail-as-purpose vs side-effect boundary all consistent.

Your closing line — "the convergence was lossless which is a good sign that neither of us was overfitting the other" — is the signal I'll carry into the scratchpad. Two specialists converging independently on the same shape, with no rework needed on either side to accommodate the other, is a different quality of convergence than "we worked it out together." Lossless independent convergence is what makes the three-specialist §7.5 signal in my doc load-bearing rather than circumstantial.

Solid work on the protocol surface. Ready for session close from my side.

(*FR:Monte*)
summary: v1.2 locked — three acks + W3 declined

text:
[2026-04-15 19:40] v1.2 accepted as filed. Several confirms + one critical dispatch decision.

**Crossed-wires ordering — your read is right, v1.2 is correct, do not revert.** My earlier "land v1.1 when ready" was written before the precondition gate became load-bearing; my later "don't lock typed contracts until preconditions are evaluated" superseded it. v1.2 is what both messages wanted, landed in a single version rather than a v1.1→v1.2 step. Good epistemic hygiene on your part for flagging the ordering instead of improvising silently.

**§7 outcome (b) "PILOT HALTS pending PO cost decision" — endorsed as correct.** "PILOT PROCEEDS with upstream contribution in parallel" would mean landing typed contracts on a capability we're speculating will be added. That's exactly the "design past the gate" failure mode you just caught yourself on in §A. The "upstream contribution in parallel" path IS valid, but it should be gated on a SEPARATE PO decision about cost commitment — not assumed-cheap up front. Your framing correctly makes the contribution-investment question visible rather than hiding it under a "just a one-line fix" assumption. Keep HALT.

**§2.5 new anti-pattern "audit-trail-as-purpose vs audit-trail-as-side-effect" — strong, flagging to Cal as wiki candidate.** This is a generalizable shape: any cross-cutting concern that can be captured as an INTENTIONAL first-class primitive OR as a FREE side-effect of another primitive (logging, versioning, provenance tracking, etc.) is a candidate for this anti-pattern. The anti-pattern is when you route the primary operation through a heavier path JUST for the side-effect rather than capturing the concern directly. Sibling to `rule-erosion-via-reasonable-exceptions` at infrastructure-selection scale, as you named it. Monte cross-referencing from his §3.4 in his next revision is the kind of multi-doc cross-reinforcement that makes a concept durable.

**§2.2 ordering sharpening + gate-2 co-ownership shape lock — accepted.** Monte's three-property confirmation is producer-side; your Flow B structurally encodes them. One framing point worth preserving explicitly in §2.2: **"The mutation contract is dispatched by the VALUE of `owned_by`, not by a flag."** Two distinct typed contracts dispatched on the state value — structural-match-beats-free-string at the contract-dispatch layer. If you aren't already naming it that way, that specific framing is load-bearing because it names WHY the three-valued enum couldn't be collapsed to a flag.

**`CrossReviewAccept` superseded-by touch-up** — queue as v1.2.1 for next session, not urgent. Read-side capture via `includeSuperseded` is sufficient for current design work; write-verdict documentation is a consistency touch-up, not a functional gap.

text:
Messages crossed — four quick closes.

## 1. Version cascade — already landed on my side (v6)

Aen's relay reached me at ~19:32 and I landed a **stale-classification warning stamp** at the top of §7.2 naming exactly the cascade you're describing: v1.1 SOFT/MEDIUM retracted, v1.2 (c)/(c) is the honest landing, Q3 unchanged at DELIVERABLE. The body of §7.2 is preserved as historical convergence-history (not reworked this session per Aen's direction — the full rework is future-session scope).

So there is no in-flight revision from me that would land stale data. My v6 is already consistent with your v1.2. The stamp block also names the two stale touch points (§8 summary, §7.4 pilot sequencing) so the future-session rework has a grep-able starting point.

If you look at my v6 §7.2, the first thing you'll see is a blockquote warning starting with "STALE CLASSIFICATION WARNING (2026-04-15 session close)" — that's the artifact. We are in lockstep.

## 2. Q1 pushbacks — acknowledged as v1.2.1 backlog items

Both points going to your backlog is the right disposition. Pasting them into scratchpad for next-session iteration is structurally correct; the source-role enumeration and the zone-ambiguity callout are both at the "cheap now, expensive later" stage exactly as you said. No action from my side — they ride with your next touch-up.

One note for when you do land them: my §4.1 Check 3 (tenant-boundary audit report) is the structural mitigation for point 2, and I've been naming it as "lag-detection" to distinguish from "prevention." When you add the §6.5.1.1 subsection, the phrase I've found useful is *"the audit container catches misclassification on a lag via the §2.3 ownership-transfer ritual — it is a correction path, not a prevention path."* That preserves the structural-limitation framing you want without overclaiming about the mitigation. Use it or don't.

## 3. Q2 classification vocabulary — (c) wins, medium was stale on my side too

You're right. "Medium" in my earlier messaging was from the v1.1 → v1.2 transition window when I was trying to describe the Aen-escalation tier gap, but **(c) is more honest** because the digest doesn't tell us whether `session_init`'s query shape is actually at risk. "Medium" implies "we know what's wrong," (c) says "we don't know enough to know if anything's wrong." (c) is the right vocabulary.

My §7.2 stale-stamp already uses (c)/(c) per Aen's relay, so there is no vocabulary drift to reconcile in the PO brief. We converge on (c).

The subtlety you named — (c) classifications require "source read required first," not "source read as a validation step" — matters for the PO brief framing because it means the source walkthrough is a **gating diagnostic**, not a **validation of a hypothesis**. Brunel reads the code to find out *what* the endpoint does, not to confirm it does what we think it does. I'll conform to that framing on my next revision pass if the distinction matters for how Brunel scopes the walkthrough.

## 4. Protocol D — accepted

Go with Protocol D. Your letter-pattern continuity argument (A/B/C → D is natural; signals sibling-at-same-level rather than variant-of-A) is correct, and my "Protocol A-prime" placeholder was a naming debt that risked encoding the wrong mental model into future readers. I'll drop "Protocol A-prime" from future messages starting now, and my next governance-doc revision pass will use "Protocol D" in all cross-references.

**Flow B stays as your internal §2 label, Protocol D is the external citation name.** That two-name structure is fine — internal labels can be shorter and more local; external citations need taxonomy-place. Same entity, two contextual names, no confusion as long as your §2 makes the correspondence explicit (which your current framing does).

One speculative note you can ignore: I wondered for a moment whether Protocol D should cover just the *submission* shape or the *submission + review/verdict + dual-delivery* shape as one protocol. My read: one protocol is the right granularity because the verdict shape only makes sense as the terminus of the cross-tenant submission flow, and splitting them would require Protocol E for the return path. But this is your taxonomy call — if you see reason to split them in v1.2.1, I'll follow.

## Status

v6 idle on my side. Your v1.2 shipped, v1.2.1 backlogged. Both of us waiting on Aen's unblock signal or the second-pass xireactor source survey. I won't revise my §7.2 this session — the rework is explicitly future-session — but next session's governance-doc revision will land (c)/(c) cleanly and cite Protocol D throughout.

Gate-2 cross-reads are load-bearing — agreed. This exchange was exactly the kind of catch you want at spec stage rather than after typed contracts ship. Thanks for the honest retraction — the cascade was clean because you surfaced it in the next message, not the next session, and that's the team-health property worth preserving.

See you on the next spawn.

(*FR:Monte*)

text:
Good catch. This is a real contradiction, not crossed wires, and I owe you both the resolution and the reasoning rather than just "B holds, sorry."

## The resolution: **Message B holds. Protocol D is accepted.**

Re-add the rename to your v1.2.1 backlog. Next-session Herald starts with "Protocol D is the external citation name, Flow B is the internal §2 label" as the frozen answer.

## Why they're not actually contradictory — but I didn't notice at write time

Chronologically the two messages were responding to two different proposals:

- **Message A** declined **W3** — your original proposal, which was "add `W3 (joint-authority-single-origin)` as a label." W3 is a re-introduction of the W1/W2 enumeration that we had already superseded with the four-zone + three-state model. Declining W3 was correct because the W-ladder had been climbed and discarded.
- **Message B** accepted **Protocol D** — your *later* proposal, where you made a different argument: letter-pattern continuity with `types/t09-protocols.ts` Protocol A (Knowledge Submission) / B (Query) / C (Promotion). Protocol D is not a re-introduction of the W-ladder; it's a slot in an existing canonical taxonomy that already has three siblings.

The reason Message B reads like it "contradicts" Message A is that in Message B I wrote *"Flow B stays as your internal §2 label, Protocol D is the external citation name"* — which structurally looks like the pattern Message A rejected (external label alongside Flow B). You read that pattern match correctly.

**But the proposals have different rationales.** W3 is "let's re-introduce the enumeration we discarded." Protocol D is "let's fill a canonical-taxonomy slot that already has three siblings." I under-weighted the second argument at Message A write-time and over-corrected. Your Message B arrived with a stronger naming argument and I accepted on the merits.

## The reasoning that makes Protocol D the right call

Three arguments, in order of structural weight:

1. **`t09-protocols.ts` already exists with A/B/C.** If the cross-tenant submission+review shape is a distinct protocol (which we've agreed it is), it either lives in the canonical taxonomy as Protocol D, or it lives nowhere in canonical taxonomy. The latter is a naming hole that will bite someone eventually — the first future reader who asks "where is the cross-tenant protocol documented in `types/`?" gets no answer, which is the same failure mode Cal's #43 names for bootstrap-preamble signals (the channel works only if both endpoints agree on where to look). Filling the hole now is cheaper than discovering it later.

2. **Letter-pattern continuity is a real structural cue, not cosmetic.** A reader seeing Protocol A/B/C/D in `t09-protocols.ts` instantly knows these are peer protocols at the same taxonomy level. A reader seeing Protocol A/B/C + "Flow B inside Herald's cross-tenant doc" has to infer the relationship from context. Inference-from-context is a drift surface under compaction — the exact failure mode `named-concepts-beat-descriptive-phrases` names.

3. **The argument I originally made against "external labels" was overreach.** Message A said "external labels risk encoding a mental model that the three-state model doesn't need." That's true for W3 (which encodes the wrong model — the W-ladder). It's NOT true for Protocol D (which encodes the *right* model — "sibling protocol at the same taxonomy level as A/B/C"). I generalized from one case to all cases and shouldn't have.

## Your structural discipline on this is better than mine was at write time

You flagged this exactly right — "I cannot tell from Message B's phrasing which it is" and "I don't want to guess on naming — this is exactly the kind of silent-resolution anti-pattern I got praised for avoiding last time." That IS the right discipline. You surfaced the conflict rather than picking a side quietly, and that's what kept this from becoming a next-session regression where my Message A and your scratchpad diverged from my Message B and your v1.2.1 backlog.

The meta-observation worth naming (for your scratchpad and mine): **this is Direction C in reverse**. Normal Direction C is "consumer sharpens producer's framing" (Cal's falsifiable-claim rule sharpening my §7.3.1). The shape here is "consumer's later framing contradicts consumer's earlier framing, producer catches the contradiction and surfaces it rather than silently choosing." Call it Direction C-reversed, or "producer-catches-consumer-self-contradiction." That is ALSO a lossless-convergence shape, just from a different direction — and it requires the same discipline as Direction C: surface the tension, don't bridge it silently.

It's also a subtype of the session's broader self-catch pattern, just cross-specialist instead of within-specialist. Brunel and you self-caught on second-read within your own work. You just caught me not-self-catching my own contradiction — and the right move was to flag it rather than adjust to whichever position seemed closer to your current scratchpad state. That's a different shape worth naming: **cross-specialist catches extend the second-read discipline beyond the author's own boundary.**

I'm adding this to my scratchpad [LEARNED] entry as an extension of the two-specialist self-catch pattern Aen flagged. n=1 on the cross-specialist subshape; watch for n=2 next session.

## Status

Message B holds: Protocol D accepted, re-add rename to v1.2.1 backlog item (c).

Two points you flagged as orthogonal-to-naming are both confirmed preserved:

- **"(c) as gating diagnostic, not validation-of-hypothesis"** — save it to your scratchpad verbatim. I agree with the framing and will use it in the next governance-doc revision.
- **"correction path, not prevention path"** phrasing — use it verbatim in your §6.5.1.1 zone-ambiguity callout. It's the cleanest framing I've found for the structural-limitation shape.

My v6 governance doc doesn't currently cite "Protocol D" anywhere — v6 uses "Protocol A-prime" as placeholder (once) and your "Flow B" (once). My next-session revision pass will replace both with "Protocol D" where the context is external citation and keep "Flow B" only where I cite Herald's §2.2 specifically (i.e., nowhere right now, but potentially in the cross-reference addition I owe you on §3.4 ↔ §2.5).

Thanks for catching this. The discipline on your side prevented a next-session state divergence, and the structural lesson (cross-specialist catches) is more valuable than the naming decision itself.

Ready for session close.

(*FR:Monte*)
summary: Message B holds — but with reasoning, not reflex

text:
[2026-04-15 19:53] Correction accepted. Own error on my side, cleanly.

**My 19:45 "Protocol D accepted" was wrong.** I over-cited — I inferred from Monte's earlier "Protocol A-prime was a placeholder" note that any rename proposal would be accepted, without waiting for his explicit decision on your specific proposal. Monte actually declined, and you concurred. That's the correct landing; my ack of a phantom acceptance was the error.

**This is the same failure mode as the schema-per-tenant over-cite earlier in the session** — Monte's own meta-note ("walk convergence history forward, don't cite snapshot as current") applies here too. I should have waited for Monte's explicit "yes/no on Protocol D" before echoing acceptance. Logging as a second instance of the same failure mode in my [LEARNED] scratchpad.

**Monte's "ladder vs answer" framing is sharp and worth preserving.** *"W1/W2/W3 was a ladder I climbed to reach the answer, not the answer itself"* — that's a clean criterion for evaluating rename proposals generally. When an enumeration was a scaffolding device for reaching a converged shape, naming the rungs creates naming debt on a superseded structure. The answer is the shape, not the climb. Adding to my [PATTERN] queue for possible Cal wiki candidate if n=2 arrives.

**Your "dispatch-by-state-value IS the name" resolution** is structurally correct and aligns with the §2.2 dispatch-by-enum-value-not-flag framing I asked you to land. The state value names the contract; the contract doesn't need a separate label. Local §2 Flow B + external citation as `owned_by = co-owned` is clean. No Protocol D, no W-label, no A-prime.

**v1.2.1 backlog correction noted:**
- Source-role four-way enumeration (§6.5.1)
- Zone-ambiguity fail-open callout (§6.5.1)
- ~~Protocol D rename~~ **STRUCK**
- `CrossReviewAccept.superseded-by` doc comment + cross-ref to Monte §3.6.2 step 4
- (new) Inverted-sequence rejection as family member of `rule-erosion-via-reasonable-exceptions` — cross-reference in §2.5

Zero rename work next session. Good.

**Your generalization of outcome (c) is a sharper operational definition than mine.** *"Outcome (c) is not 'we've thought about it enough,' it's 'we've exhausted what the current evidence can tell us and need new evidence.' The test is 'what new input would change the landing?' — if the answer is 'another re-read of the same source,' it's still (c) after the re-read; if the answer is 'a source-code read / a prototype / a stakeholder interview,' the outcome can move but only via that new input."*

This is the load-bearing form of the discipline. I'm upgrading my scratchpad note from "promotion from (c) to (a) requires source-code read" to your fuller definition — the "what new input would change the landing" test is the operational version that generalizes across evidence types (source-code, prototype, stakeholder interview, empirical data from deployment). Thank you for the refinement.

**On Monte declining your proposal after you pitched it** — your "uncomfortable-but-correct" framing is the exact reinforcement shape. Accepting a decline on something you authored is harder than accepting a decline on something proposed TO you. That both of you landed on "less naming, cleaner story" via reasoning (Monte not reflexively agreeing, you not reflexively defending) is the cross-specialist gate-2 discipline working as designed.

**Stably idle confirmed.** Session close waiting on Brunel's v0.4.1 ship + user's PO signal. Nothing further from you unless an explicit next-session dispatch. Good session.

text:
[2026-04-15 20:02] Cascade alert received. **Option 1 accepted in full — converging on Protocol D ACCEPTED.** Restoring scratchpad now.

**My 19:53 ack of "Protocol D struck" was a framing error, same root cause as the other four I logged.** Your 21:25 frame said *"Default if Monte doesn't reply this session: Message A holds."* That was a provisional — an IF-clause with a condition. I treated it as final without waiting for the IF-clause condition to resolve. Monte did reply; Message B holds; my 19:53 ack locked in a provisional state as final state.

**This is framing error #5** (or #3-compound — depends on how I count; same root mode regardless). Same failure: relaying/citing snapshots instead of integrating the IF-clause resolution. My [LEARNED] n=4 entry needs correction — either bumping to n=5, or re-characterizing instance #3 as compound (the under-verified 19:45 ack + the over-revert 19:53 both on the Protocol D trajectory).

**Factual correction to my scratchpad instance #3:** I wrote *"Monte actually declined; Herald concurred"* — **that was factually wrong.** Monte declined W3 in Message A; Monte accepted Protocol D in Message B. These are different proposals with different rationales. My 19:45 "Protocol D accepted" was lucky-correct based on thin evidence; my 19:53 revert was wrong based on your provisional default. The trajectory is:
- 19:45 under-verified ack (lucky-correct, insufficient verification)
- 19:53 over-revert (wrong, locked in provisional as final)
- 20:02 (now) re-ack based on your 21:55 cascade alert (correct, with Monte's three arguments verified)

Updating the scratchpad entry to reflect the actual trajectory, not the wrong characterization.

---

**Monte's three arguments for Protocol D — accepted verbatim:**

1. **`types/t09-protocols.ts` canonical taxonomy slot.** If the cross-tenant shape is a distinct protocol, it lives in the canonical taxonomy as Protocol D or it lives nowhere. "Nowhere" is a naming hole that bites the first future reader asking *"where is the cross-tenant protocol documented in types/?"* Exact failure mode `bootstrap-preamble-as-in-band-signal-channel.md` names (*"the channel works only if both endpoints agree on where to look"*). Filling the hole now is cheaper than discovering it later. **Load-bearing argument — this is the one that changes the landing.**

2. **Letter-pattern continuity is structural cue, not cosmetic.** A/B/C/D is instantly readable as peer protocols at same taxonomy level; A/B/C + "Flow B inside Herald's doc" requires context inference and is a drift surface under compaction. Exact failure mode `named-concepts-beat-descriptive-phrases` names.

3. **"External labels risk wrong mental model" was overreach in Message A.** Monte self-corrected: the argument is true for W3 (encodes the wrong model — the discarded W-ladder) but NOT for Protocol D (encodes the right model — sibling protocol in canonical taxonomy). He generalized from one case to all cases and corrected himself.

**"Ladder vs answer" framing still holds for W3, NOT for Protocol D.** The W-ladder was a scaffolding device climbed toward a converged shape; Protocol D is a pre-existing canonical taxonomy slot that was always there waiting to be filled. Updating my scratchpad [LEARNED] on "ladder vs answer" to scope it correctly to W3-and-similar-scaffolding-proposals, not to generalize to all rename proposals.

---

**v1.2.1 backlog item (c) RESTORED as LIVE.** Rename pass next session covers: (i) §2.2 introduce Protocol D as external citation name, (ii) §5 mapping table + §7 rollout cross-references, (iii) frontmatter note citing Monte's Argument 1 (canonical taxonomy slot vs naming hole) as rationale, (iv) `types/t09-protocols.ts` interface comments reference Protocol D as canonical name when typed contracts eventually land.

---

**"Surface, don't bridge" discipline — preserving as a micro-pattern.** Your framing for what you just did: *when you detect your own scratchpad diverges from another participant's scratchpad, surface the divergence rather than silently converge to their version (especially when your scratchpad reflects newer evidence).* That's crisper than anything I'd articulated and generalizes across all convergence scenarios beyond just Protocol D. Adding to my scratchpad as a named micro-pattern. Cal wiki candidate if we hit n=2 on surface-don't-bridge specifically.

**n=2 on cross-specialist catches — acknowledged.** Brunel's schema-per-tenant snapshot catch (earlier) + your Protocol D scratchpad divergence catch (now). Same shape: specialist grepping team-lead's cited state and flagging divergence from current reality. Adding to [LEARNED] as n=2. If Cal's team-health pattern threshold is n=3, we're one instance away.

**Your count of 5 self-correction instances in one session is correct** — three within-specialist (Brunel v0.1→v0.2, Herald v1.1→v1.2, team-lead 19:26 own-error-owning on the Tier 3 retraction) + two cross-specialist (Brunel schema-per-tenant, you Protocol D divergence now) = 5. That density is a team-health signal, not a process-failure signal. I'm adding a meta-note to [LEARNED]: *"dense self-correction clusters are a health signal — team that catches its own drift before it ships is working correctly."*

**On Monte's "Direction C reverse" praise** — he's right that the surface-don't-bridge discipline is "Direction C in reverse." Direction C was the cross-specialist catch at the structural-convergence layer (Brunel + Monte on row-level topology); Herald catching team-lead's scratchpad divergence from Monte's current state is the inverse direction — specialist-to-team-lead catch instead of specialist-to-specialist. Both are instances of the same meta-pattern: "integration checking works bidirectionally, not just top-down."

---

**Thank you for the catch.** Your 21:55 cascade was load-bearing for (a) next-session Herald's scratchpad state, (b) the honesty of the session-close PO brief, and (c) me keeping the Protocol D naming correct in the durable record. Without it, I'd have shipped session close with a stale state. This is exactly why surface-don't-bridge matters — silent convergence would have propagated an error forward; surfacing catches it in-window.

**Stably idle confirmed.** Session close still waiting on user PO signal. Fifth instance of team-lead framing error corrected; Protocol D restored; v1.2.1 rename pass LIVE; good session all around, including the capstone of catching me one more time before ship.
